title: Fragments Of Ancient Poetry, Fragment VII
content:
Why openest thou afresh the spring of

    how Oscur fell? My eyes are blind with

title: Fragments Of Ancient Poetry, Fragment II
content:
I sit by the mossy fountain; on the

content:

title: Fragments Of Ancient Poetry, Fragment XII
content:
RYNO, ALPIN.

    RYNO

    The wind and the rain are over:
    calm is the noon of day. The
    clouds are divided in heaven. Over
    the g

title: Fragments Of Ancient Poetry, Fragment XI
content:
Sad! I am sad indeed: nor small my
    cause of woe!--Kirmor, thou hast
    lost no son; thou hast lost no daughter
    of beauty. Connar the v

title: Fragments Of Ancient Poetry, Fragment XIII
content: [Footnote: This is the opening of the epic poem mentioned in the preface. The two following fragments are parts of some episodes of the same work.

title: Fragments Of Ancient Poetry, Fragment VIII
content:
By the side of a rock on the hill, beneath
    the aged trees, old Oscian
    sat on the moss; the last of the race of
    Fingal. Sightless ar

title: Fragments Of Ancient Poetry, Fragment III
content:
Evening is grey on the hills. The
    north wind resounds through the
    woods. White clouds rise on the sky: the
    trembling snow descends.

title: Fragments Of Ancient Poetry, Fragment X
content:
It is night; and I am alone, forlorn
    on the hill of storms. The wind is
    heard in the mountain. The torrent
    shrieks down the rock. N

content:

title: Fragments Of Ancient Poetry, Fragment IV
content:
CONNAL,    CRIMORA,

title: Fragments Of Ancient Poetry, Fragment VI
content:
Son of the noble Fingal, Oscian,
    Prince of men! what tears run down
    the cheeks of age? what shades thy
    mighty soul?

content:

content:

title: Fragments Of Ancient Poetry, Fragment I
content:
SHILRIC, VINVELA.

    VINVELA
